If the argument is absent, the default is `false` (secure). 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - The node is compliant if `--peer-auto-tls` is not present or explicitly set to `false`. - Document the findings for audit purposes.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that a unique Certificate Authority is used for etcd
description: Kubernetes	can	audit	the	details	of	requests	made	to	the	API	server.	The	--audit-policy- file	flag	must	be	set	for	this	logging	to	be	enabled.
rationale: Logging	is	an	important	detective	control	for	all	systems,	to	detect	potential	unauthorised access. Impact: Audit	logs	will	be	created	on	the	master	nodes,	which	will	consume	disk	space.	Care	should be	taken	to	avoid	generating	too	large	volumes	of	log	information	as	this	could	impact	the available	of	the	cluster	nodes.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	one	of	the	cluster	master	nodes: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify	that	the	--audit-policy-file	is	set.	Review	the	contents	of	the	file	specified	and ensure	that	it	contains	a	valid	audit	policy.
remediation: Create	an	audit	policy	file	for	your	cluster. Default	Value: Unless	the	--audit-policy-file	flag	is	specified,	no	auditing	will	be	carried	out. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/ 2. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/91406.htm 156	|	P a g e Additional	Information: This	control	cannot	be	audited	and	modified	in	ACK	managed	clusters. CIS	Controls: Version	7 6.2	Activate	audit	logging Ensure	that	local	logging	has	been	enabled	on	all	systems	and	networking	devices. 157	|	P a g e
impact: Audit	logs	will	be	created	on	the	master	nodes,	which	will	consume	disk	space.	Care	should be	taken	to	avoid	generating	too	large	volumes	of	log	information	as	this	could	impact	the available	of	the	cluster	nodes.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/ 2. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/91406.htm 156	|	P a g e Additional	Information: This	control	cannot	be	audited	and	modified	in	ACK	managed	clusters. CIS	Controls: Version	7 6.2	Activate	audit	logging Ensure	that	local	logging	has	been	enabled	on	all	systems	and	networking	devices. 157	|	P a g e
justification: The audit requires verifying the presence and content of the `--audit-policy-file` flag in the kube-apiserver process on the master node. This information is not exposed via Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or ACK managed service endpoints. Direct access to the underlying host (via SSH) is necessary to inspect process arguments and file contents, which cannot be achieved through any Alibaba Cloud automation or API. Both AI opinions acknowledge the need for manual inspection, and the CIS benchmark explicitly states this control cannot be audited or modified in ACK managed clusters through automated means.
manual_summary: 1. **Obtain SSH Access:** - Use the Alibaba Cloud Console to retrieve SSH credentials for a Kubernetes master node in your ACK cluster. - SSH into the master node. 2. **Check kube-apiserver Process:** - Run the command: ``` ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver ``` - Look for the `--audit-policy-file` flag in the process arguments. 3. **Verify Audit Policy File:** - Note the path specified for `--audit-policy-file`. - Open and review the contents of the specified file to ensure it contains a valid Kubernetes audit policy as per your organizational requirements. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Compliance is achieved if the `--audit-policy-file` flag is present and the referenced file contains a valid audit policy. - If the flag is missing or the file is invalid/missing, the cluster is non-compliant.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the audit policy covers key security concerns
description: Ensure	that	the	audit	policy	created	for	the	cluster	covers	key	security	concerns.
rationale: Security	audit	logs	should	cover	access	and	modification	of	key	resources	in	the	cluster,	to enable	them	to	form	an	effective	part	of	a	security	environment. Impact: Increasing	audit	logging	will	consume	resources	on	the	nodes	or	other	log	destination.
audit: Review	the	audit	policy	provided	for	the	cluster	and	ensure	that	it	covers	at	least	the following	areas	:- • Access	to	Secrets	managed	by	the	cluster.	Care	should	be	taken	to	only	log	Metadata for	requests	to	Secrets,	ConfigMaps,	and	TokenReviews,	in	order	to	avoid	the	risk	of logging	sensitive	data. • Modification	of	pod	and	deployment	objects. • Use	of	pods/exec,	pods/portforward,	pods/proxy	and	services/proxy. For	most	requests,	minimally	logging	at	the	Metadata	level	is	recommended	(the	most	basic level	of	logging).
remediation: Consider	modification	of	the	audit	policy	in	use	on	the	cluster	to	include	these	items,	at	a minimum. Default	Value: By	default	Kubernetes	clusters	do	not	log	audit	information. References: 158	|	P a g e 1. https://github.com/k8scop/k8s-security- dashboard/blob/master/configs/kubernetes/adv-audit.yaml 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/#audit-policy 3. https://github.com/falcosecurity/falco/blob/master/examples/k8s_audit_config/a udit-policy.yaml 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/cluster/gce/gci/configur e-helper.sh#L735 5. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/91406.htm Additional	Information: This	control	cannot	be	audited	and	modified	in	ACK	managed	clusters. CIS	Controls: Version	6 14.6	Enforce	Detailed	Audit	Logging	For	Sensitive	Information Enforce	detailed	audit	logging	for	access	to	nonpublic	data	and	special	authentication	for sensitive	data. Version	7 14.9	Enforce	Detail	Logging	for	Access	or	Changes	to	Sensitive	Data Enforce	detailed	audit	logging	for	access	to	sensitive	data	or	changes	to	sensitive	data (utilizing	tools	such	as	File	Integrity	Monitoring	or	Security	Information	and	Event Monitoring). 4 Worker Nodes This	section	consists	of	security	recommendations	for	the	components	that	run	on Kubernetes	worker	nodes. Note	that	these	components	may	also	run	on	Kubernetes	master	nodes,	so	the recommendations	in	this	section	should	be	applied	to	master	nodes	as	well	as	worker nodes	where	the	master	nodes	make	use	of	these	components. 159	|	P a g e 4.1 Worker Node Configuration Files This	section	covers	recommendations	for	configuration	files	on	the	worker	nodes. To	Perform	an	Automated	Audit	utilizing	CIS-CAT	the	following	parameters	must	be	set	on each	node	being	evaluated. $kubelet_service_config $kubelet_config $kubelet_config_yaml If	you	are	auditing	a	kubeadm	environment	the	default	settings	for	these	values	are	below: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf export kubelet_config=/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf export kubelet_config_yaml=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
impact: Increasing	audit	logging	will	consume	resources	on	the	nodes	or	other	log	destination.
references: 158	|	P a g e 1. https://github.com/k8scop/k8s-security- dashboard/blob/master/configs/kubernetes/adv-audit.yaml 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/#audit-policy 3. https://github.com/falcosecurity/falco/blob/master/examples/k8s_audit_config/a udit-policy.yaml 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/cluster/gce/gci/configur e-helper.sh#L735 5. https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/doc-detail/91406.htm Additional	Information: This	control	cannot	be	audited	and	modified	in	ACK	managed	clusters. CIS	Controls: Version	6 14.6	Enforce	Detailed	Audit	Logging	For	Sensitive	Information Enforce	detailed	audit	logging	for	access	to	nonpublic	data	and	special	authentication	for sensitive	data. Version	7 14.9	Enforce	Detail	Logging	for	Access	or	Changes	to	Sensitive	Data Enforce	detailed	audit	logging	for	access	to	sensitive	data	or	changes	to	sensitive	data (utilizing	tools	such	as	File	Integrity	Monitoring	or	Security	Information	and	Event Monitoring). 4 Worker Nodes This	section	consists	of	security	recommendations	for	the	components	that	run	on Kubernetes	worker	nodes. Note	that	these	components	may	also	run	on	Kubernetes	master	nodes,	so	the recommendations	in	this	section	should	be	applied	to	master	nodes	as	well	as	worker nodes	where	the	master	nodes	make	use	of	these	components. 159	|	P a g e 4.1 Worker Node Configuration Files This	section	covers	recommendations	for	configuration	files	on	the	worker	nodes. To	Perform	an	Automated	Audit	utilizing	CIS-CAT	the	following	parameters	must	be	set	on each	node	being	evaluated. $kubelet_service_config $kubelet_config $kubelet_config_yaml If	you	are	auditing	a	kubeadm	environment	the	default	settings	for	these	values	are	below: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf export kubelet_config=/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf export kubelet_config_yaml=/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
justification: While some aspects of Kubernetes cluster configuration can be queried via Alibaba Cloud APIs or CLI (such as cluster details), the specific content and coverage of the audit policy—especially the nuanced evaluation of whether it addresses key security concerns (e.g., logging access to Secrets, ConfigMaps, TokenReviews, and specific pod operations at the correct granularity)—requires human judgment and direct inspection of the policy file. Alibaba Cloud APIs do not expose the full audit policy content or its detailed rules for ACK clusters, nor do they provide a way to programmatically assess the qualitative adequacy of those rules. Therefore, a manual review is necessary to ensure compliance.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Alibaba Cloud Console:** - Log in to the Alibaba Cloud Console. - Navigate to "Container Service for Kubernetes" (ACK). - Select the target Kubernetes cluster. 2. **Locate the Audit Policy Configuration:** - Access the cluster’s configuration section. - Identify and download the audit policy file in use (typically `audit-policy.yaml` or similar). - If the audit policy is not visible in the console, access the master node (if permitted) or request the policy from the cluster administrator. 3. **Review Audit Policy Content:** - Open the audit policy file. - Verify that it includes rules for: - Logging access to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews (ensure only metadata is logged for sensitive resources). - Logging modification events for pods and deployments. - Logging use of pods/exec, pods/portforward, pods/proxy, and services/proxy. - Ensure the logging level is set to at least "Metadata" for sensitive resources to avoid exposure of sensitive data. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - The policy must explicitly cover all key areas listed above. - Document the presence and adequacy of each required rule. - If any area is missing or insufficiently covered, mark as non-compliant.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
description: Ensure	that	the	kubelet	service	file	has	permissions	of	644	or	more	restrictive.
rationale: The	kubelet	service	file	controls	various	parameters	that	set	the	behavior	of	the	kubelet service	in	the	worker	node.	You	should	restrict	its	file	permissions	to	maintain	the	integrity of	the	file.	The	file	should	be	writable	by	only	the	administrators	on	the	system. Impact: None
audit: 160	|	P a g e Automated	AAC	auditing	has	been	modified	to	allow	CIS-CAT	to	input	a	variable	for	the	/	of the	kubelet	service	config	file. Please	set	$kubelet_service_config=	based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system for	example: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf To	perform	the	audit	manually: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	each	worker node.	For	example, stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Verify	that	the	permissions	are	644	or	more	restrictive.
remediation: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	each	worker node.	For	example, chmod 755 /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Default	Value: By	default,	the	kubelet	service	file	has	permissions	of	640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 161	|	P a g e 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 162	|	P a g e
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 161	|	P a g e 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 162	|	P a g e
justification: This control requires verification of file permissions on the kubelet service file located on the underlying operating system of each ACK worker node. Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or Config do not expose OS-level file permission details for managed Kubernetes nodes. Both AI opinions correctly identify that direct OS access (typically via SSH) is necessary to check these permissions, and automation via Alibaba Cloud-native tools is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. **Access Each Worker Node:** - SSH into every worker node in your Alibaba Cloud ACK cluster using administrative credentials. 2. **Identify Kubelet Service File:** - Determine the location of the kubelet service configuration file (commonly `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf`). 3. **Check File Permissions:** - Run the following command to check the file permissions: ```bash stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf ``` 4. **Verify Compliance:** - Ensure the output is `644` or a more restrictive value (e.g., `640`, `600`). Any value more permissive than `644` (such as `666` or `777`) is non-compliant. 5. **Repeat for All Nodes:** - Perform this check on every worker node to ensure cluster-wide compliance.

title: Ensure that the kubelet service file ownership is set to root:root
description: Ensure	that	the	kubelet	service	file	ownership	is	set	to	root:root.
rationale: The	kubelet	service	file	controls	various	parameters	that	set	the	behavior	of	the	kubelet service	in	the	worker	node.	You	should	set	its	file	ownership	to	maintain	the	integrity	of	the file.	The	file	should	be	owned	by	root:root. Impact: None
audit: Automated	AAC	auditing	has	been	modified	to	allow	CIS-CAT	to	input	a	variable	for	the	/	of the	kubelet	service	config	file. Please	set	$kubelet_service_config=	based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system for	example: export kubelet_service_config=/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf To	perform	the	audit	manually: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	each	worker node.	For	example, stat -c %a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Verify	that	the	ownership	is	set	to	root:root.
remediation: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	each	worker node.	For	example, 163	|	P a g e chown root:root /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf Default	Value: By	default,	kubelet	service	file	ownership	is	set	to	root:root. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/#44- joining-your-nodes 3. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubeadm/#kubelet-drop-in CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 164	|	P a g e

Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 164	|	P a g e
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control requires verification of file ownership on the underlying operating system of each Kubernetes worker node. Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or Config do not expose file-level ownership or permissions for files within ECS instances or managed ACK nodes. This check necessitates direct OS-level access (typically via SSH) to inspect and potentially remediate the kubelet service file ownership, which cannot be achieved through Alibaba Cloud’s native automation or API tools.
manual_summary: 1. **Access Each Worker Node:** - SSH into each Kubernetes worker node in your cluster. 2. **Locate the Kubelet Service File:** - Identify the kubelet service configuration file path (commonly `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` or as set in your environment). 3. **Verify File Ownership:** - Run the following command to check ownership: `stat -c %U:%G /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` - The output must be `root:root`. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The file must be owned by `root` user and `root` group. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - If the ownership is not `root:root`, the node is non-compliant. - Remediate by running: `chown root:root /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` - Re-run the verification command to confirm compliance.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: If proxy kubeconfig file exists ensure permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
description: If	kube-proxy	is	running,	and	if	it	is	using	a	file-based	kubeconfig	file,	ensure	that	the	proxy kubeconfig	file	has	permissions	of	644	or	more	restrictive.
rationale: The	kube-proxy	kubeconfig	file	controls	various	parameters	of	the	kube-proxy	service	in the	worker	node.	You	should	restrict	its	file	permissions	to	maintain	the	integrity	of	the	file. The	file	should	be	writable	by	only	the	administrators	on	the	system. It	is	possible	to	run	kube-proxy	with	the	kubeconfig	parameters	configured	as	a Kubernetes	ConfigMap	instead	of	a	file.	In	this	case,	there	is	no	proxy	kubeconfig	file. Impact: None
audit: Find	the	kubeconfig	file	being	used	by	kube-proxy	by	running	the	following	command: ps -ef | grep kube-proxy If	kube-proxy	is	running,	get	the	kubeconfig	file	location	from	the	--kubeconfig	parameter. To	perform	the	audit: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	each	worker node.	For	example, stat -c %a <path><filename> Verify	that	a	file	is	specified	and	it	exists	with	permissions	are	644	or	more	restrictive.
remediation: Run	the	below	command	(based	on	the	file	location	on	your	system)	on	the	each	worker node.	For	example, 165	|	P a g e chmod 644 <proxy kubeconfig file> Default	Value: By	default,	proxy	file	has	permissions	of	640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 166	|	P a g e
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-proxy/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 166	|	P a g e
justification: This control requires verification of file permissions for the kube-proxy kubeconfig file on each worker node. Alibaba Cloud APIs, Aliyun CLI, and Alibaba Cloud Config do not provide access to operating system-level file permissions within ECS or ACK worker nodes. The only way to reliably check and remediate this is by logging into each node and using OS-level commands. Both AI opinions recognize the lack of API/CLI support for this check, and while automation with configuration management tools is possible, it falls outside the scope of Alibaba Cloud native automation and still requires manual or custom script execution on the OS.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each worker node in your ACK cluster where kube-proxy is running. 2. Run the following command to identify the kubeconfig file path used by kube-proxy: ```bash ps -ef | grep kube-proxy ``` - Look for the `--kubeconfig` parameter in the process arguments to determine the file path. 3. Verify the permissions of the kubeconfig file with: ```bash stat -c %a <path_to_kubeconfig_file> ``` 4. Confirm that the file permissions are 644 or more restrictive (i.e., 644, 640, 600, etc.). 5. If permissions are too permissive, remediate with: ```bash chmod 644 <path_to_kubeconfig_file> ``` 6. Document the results for each node.

description: Disable	anonymous	requests	to	the	Kubelet	server.
rationale: When	enabled,	requests	that	are	not	rejected	by	other	configured	authentication	methods are	treated	as	anonymous	requests.	These	requests	are	then	served	by	the	Kubelet	server. You	should	rely	on	authentication	to	authorize	access	and	disallow	anonymous	requests. Impact: Anonymous	requests	will	be	rejected.
audit: If	using	a	Kubelet	configuration	file,	check	that	there	is	an	entry	for	authentication: anonymous: enabled	set	to	false. Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet 181	|	P a g e Verify	that	the	--anonymous-auth	argument	is	set	to	false. This	executable	argument	may	be	omitted,	provided	there	is	a	corresponding	entry	set	to false	in	the	Kubelet	config	file.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	authentication: anonymous: enabled	to false. If	using	executable	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS	variable. --anonymous-auth=false Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	anonymous	access	is	enabled. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication CIS	Controls: Version	6 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 182	|	P a g e
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication CIS	Controls: Version	6 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 182	|	P a g e
justification: The audit requires direct inspection of the kubelet process arguments or configuration files on each node to verify that `--anonymous-auth=false` is set, or that the kubelet config file disables anonymous authentication. Alibaba Cloud APIs, ACK console, or Kubernetes API do not expose the underlying kubelet process arguments or detailed kubelet configuration for managed nodes. Therefore, this check cannot be reliably automated using Alibaba Cloud or Kubernetes APIs and requires manual access to each node.
manual_summary: 1. **Connect to Each Node:** - Use SSH to connect to each worker node in your ACK cluster. 2. **Inspect Running Kubelet Process:** - Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to view the kubelet process arguments. - Confirm that `--anonymous-auth=false` is present. 3. **Check Kubelet Configuration File (if used):** - Locate the kubelet configuration file (commonly `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`). - Open the file and ensure under `authentication:` that `anonymous: enabled` is set to `false`. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Either the process argument `--anonymous-auth=false` is set, or in the config file, `authentication.anonymous.enabled: false`. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the findings for each node. Compliance is achieved only if all nodes have anonymous authentication disabled.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow
description: Do	not	allow	all	requests.	Enable	explicit	authorization.
rationale: Kubelets,	by	default,	allow	all	authenticated	requests	(even	anonymous	ones)	without needing	explicit	authorization	checks	from	the	apiserver.	You	should	restrict	this	behavior and	only	allow	explicitly	authorized	requests. Impact: Unauthorized	requests	will	be	denied.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If	the	--authorization-mode	argument	is	present	check	that	it	is	not	set	to	AlwaysAllow.	If it	is	not	present	check	that	there	is	a	Kubelet	config	file	specified	by	--config,	and	that	file sets	authorization: mode	to	something	other	than	AlwaysAllow. It	is	also	possible	to	review	the	running	configuration	of	a	Kubelet	via	the	/configz endpoint	on	the	Kubelet	API	port	(typically	10250/TCP).	Accessing	these	with	appropriate credentials	will	provide	details	of	the	Kubelet's	configuration.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	authorization: mode	to	Webhook. If	using	executable	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS	variable. --authorization-mode=Webhook Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: 183	|	P a g e systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	--authorization-mode	argument	is	set	to	AlwaysAllow. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication CIS	Controls: Version	6 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 184	|	P a g e
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication CIS	Controls: Version	6 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 184	|	P a g e
justification: The kubelet’s `--authorization-mode` setting is not exposed via Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or ACK console interfaces. Verifying this configuration requires direct inspection of process arguments or configuration files on each node, which necessitates SSH access. While some node metadata can be retrieved via Alibaba Cloud APIs, the specific kubelet runtime parameters and config files are not accessible through these means.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into Each Node:** - Use SSH to access every node in your ACK cluster where kubelet is running. 2. **Check Kubelet Process Arguments:** - Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to list the kubelet process and its arguments. - Verify that the `--authorization-mode` argument is present and is NOT set to `AlwaysAllow`. 3. **Check Kubelet Config File (if applicable):** - If the `--authorization-mode` argument is absent, check if the kubelet is started with a `--config` parameter. - Open the referenced config file and ensure `authorization.mode` is NOT set to `AlwaysAllow`. 4. **(Optional) Review /configz Endpoint:** - Access `https://<node-ip>:10250/configz` with proper credentials to review the live kubelet configuration. - Confirm that the authorization mode is not `AlwaysAllow`. 5. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - `--authorization-mode` should be set to `Webhook` or another secure mode, never `AlwaysAllow`. 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document findings for each node. Compliance is achieved if no kubelet process or config uses `AlwaysAllow`.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --client-ca-file argument is set as appropriate
description: Enable	Kubelet	authentication	using	certificates.
rationale: The	connections	from	the	apiserver	to	the	kubelet	are	used	for	fetching	logs	for	pods, attaching	(through	kubectl)	to	running	pods,	and	using	the	kubelet’s	port-forwarding functionality.	These	connections	terminate	at	the	kubelet’s	HTTPS	endpoint.	By	default,	the apiserver	does	not	verify	the	kubelet’s	serving	certificate,	which	makes	the	connection subject	to	man-in-the-middle	attacks,	and	unsafe	to	run	over	untrusted	and/or	public networks.	Enabling	Kubelet	certificate	authentication	ensures	that	the	apiserver	could authenticate	the	Kubelet	before	submitting	any	requests. Impact: You	require	TLS	to	be	configured	on	apiserver	as	well	as	kubelets.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify	that	the	--client-ca-file	argument	exists	and	is	set	to	the	location	of	the	client certificate	authority	file. If	the	--client-ca-file	argument	is	not	present,	check	that	there	is	a	Kubelet	config	file specified	by	--config,	and	that	the	file	sets	authentication: x509: clientCAFile	to	the location	of	the	client	certificate	authority	file.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	authentication: x509: clientCAFile	to the	location	of	the	client	CA	file. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file 185	|	P a g e /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS	variable. --client-ca-file=<path/to/client-ca-file> Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	--client-ca-file	argument	is	not	set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet- authentication-authorization/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 14.2	Encrypt	All	Sensitive	Information	Over	Less-trusted	Networks All	communication	of	sensitive	information	over	less-trusted	networks	should	be encrypted.	Whenever	information	flows	over	a	network	with	a	lower	trust	level,	the information	should	be	encrypted. Version	7 14.4	Encrypt	All	Sensitive	Information	in	Transit Encrypt	all	sensitive	information	in	transit. 186	|	P a g e
impact: You	require	TLS	to	be	configured	on	apiserver	as	well	as	kubelets.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet- authentication-authorization/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 14.2	Encrypt	All	Sensitive	Information	Over	Less-trusted	Networks All	communication	of	sensitive	information	over	less-trusted	networks	should	be encrypted.	Whenever	information	flows	over	a	network	with	a	lower	trust	level,	the information	should	be	encrypted. Version	7 14.4	Encrypt	All	Sensitive	Information	in	Transit Encrypt	all	sensitive	information	in	transit. 186	|	P a g e
justification: Verification of the `--client-ca-file` argument for Kubelet requires direct inspection of node-level process arguments or configuration files, which are not exposed via Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or managed Kubernetes interfaces. Both AI opinions acknowledge the lack of direct API support; while automation via DaemonSets is theoretically possible, it still requires manual setup and does not leverage Alibaba Cloud-native automation or compliance tooling. Therefore, the only reliable and supportable method is manual inspection on each node.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into Each Worker Node:** Use SSH to access each Kubernetes worker node in your ACK cluster. 2. **Check Kubelet Process Arguments:** Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to view the running Kubelet process and its arguments. - Confirm that the `--client-ca-file=<path>` argument is present and points to the correct client CA file. 3. **Check Kubelet Config File (if applicable):** If the `--client-ca-file` argument is not present, check if the Kubelet is started with a `--config <config-file>` argument. - Open the referenced config file and verify that under `authentication.x509.clientCAFile` the correct path to the client CA file is set. 4. **Validate Configuration:** Ensure the specified client CA file exists at the given path and is readable by the Kubelet process. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** Compliance is achieved if either the process argument or the config file correctly specifies the client CA file. Non-compliance if neither is set.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Verify that the --read-only-port argument is set to 0
description: Disable	the	read-only	port.
rationale: The	Kubelet	process	provides	a	read-only	API	in	addition	to	the	main	Kubelet	API. Unauthenticated	access	is	provided	to	this	read-only	API	which	could	possibly	retrieve potentially	sensitive	information	about	the	cluster. Impact: Removal	of	the	read-only	port	will	require	that	any	service	which	made	use	of	it	will	need to	be	re-configured	to	use	the	main	Kubelet	API.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify	that	the	--read-only-port	argument	exists	and	is	set	to	0. If	the	--read-only-port	argument	is	not	present,	check	that	there	is	a	Kubelet	config	file specified	by	--config.	Check	that	if	there	is	a	readOnlyPort	entry	in	the	file,	it	is	set	to	0.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	readOnlyPort	to	0. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS	variable. --read-only-port=0 Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service 187	|	P a g e Default	Value: By	default,	--read-only-port	is	set	to	10255/TCP.	However,	if	a	config	file	is	specified	by	-- config	the	default	value	for	readOnlyPort	is	0. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 9.1	Limit	Open	Ports,	Protocols,	and	Services Ensure	that	only	ports,	protocols,	and	services	with	validated	business	needs	are	running on	each	system. Version	7 9.2	Ensure	Only	Approved	Ports,	Protocols	and	Services	Are	Running Ensure	that	only	network	ports,	protocols,	and	services	listening	on	a	system	with validated	business	needs,	are	running	on	each	system. 188	|	P a g e
impact: Removal	of	the	read-only	port	will	require	that	any	service	which	made	use	of	it	will	need to	be	re-configured	to	use	the	main	Kubelet	API.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 9.1	Limit	Open	Ports,	Protocols,	and	Services Ensure	that	only	ports,	protocols,	and	services	with	validated	business	needs	are	running on	each	system. Version	7 9.2	Ensure	Only	Approved	Ports,	Protocols	and	Services	Are	Running Ensure	that	only	network	ports,	protocols,	and	services	listening	on	a	system	with validated	business	needs,	are	running	on	each	system. 188	|	P a g e
justification: Verifying that the Kubelet `--read-only-port` argument is set to 0 requires direct inspection of each node's process arguments or configuration files. Alibaba Cloud's APIs, CLI, and ACK management interfaces do not expose Kubelet process arguments or configuration file contents at the node level. This check necessitates SSH access to each node to run commands or review files, which cannot be performed via Alibaba Cloud's native automation or configuration APIs.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into Each Node:** - Use the Alibaba Cloud ECS console or an SSH client with appropriate credentials to access each Kubernetes worker node in the ACK cluster. 2. **Check Kubelet Process Arguments:** - Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to list the Kubelet process and its arguments. - Look for the `--read-only-port` argument. If present, confirm it is set to `0`. 3. **Check Kubelet Configuration File (if needed):** - If the `--read-only-port` argument is not present in the process, check if the Kubelet is started with a `--config` flag. - Open the referenced Kubelet configuration file (commonly `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf` or as specified). - Locate the `readOnlyPort` entry and confirm it is set to `0`. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - The node is compliant if either the process argument or the configuration file explicitly sets the read-only port to `0`.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout argument is not set to 0
description: Do	not	disable	timeouts	on	streaming	connections.
rationale: Setting	idle	timeouts	ensures	that	you	are	protected	against	Denial-of-Service	attacks, inactive	connections	and	running	out	of	ephemeral	ports. Note: By	default,	--streaming-connection-idle-timeout	is	set	to	4	hours	which	might	be too	high	for	your	environment.	Setting	this	as	appropriate	would	additionally	ensure	that such	streaming	connections	are	timed	out	after	serving	legitimate	use	cases. Impact: Long-lived	connections	could	be	interrupted.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify	that	the	--streaming-connection-idle-timeout	argument	is	not	set	to	0. If	the	argument	is	not	present,	and	there	is	a	Kubelet	config	file	specified	by	--config, check	that	it	does	not	set	streamingConnectionIdleTimeout	to	0.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	streamingConnectionIdleTimeout	to	a value	other	than	0. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS	variable. --streaming-connection-idle-timeout=5m 189	|	P a g e Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	--streaming-connection-idle-timeout	is	set	to	4	hours. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/18552 CIS	Controls: Version	6 9	Limitation	and	Control	of	Network	Ports,	Protocols,	and	Services Limitation	and	Control	of	Network	Ports,	Protocols,	and	Services Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 190	|	P a g e
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/18552 CIS	Controls: Version	6 9	Limitation	and	Control	of	Network	Ports,	Protocols,	and	Services Limitation	and	Control	of	Network	Ports,	Protocols,	and	Services Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 190	|	P a g e
justification: The verification of the `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout` argument for kubelet requires direct inspection of node-level configurations or process arguments, which are not exposed via Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or ACK console. Both AI opinions acknowledge that node-level access (e.g., SSH) is necessary, and while log aggregation could theoretically offer partial automation, Alibaba Cloud does not natively expose kubelet flags or config file parameters through any managed API or service. Therefore, only manual inspection can guarantee accurate compliance verification.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into each Kubernetes worker node** in your ACK cluster. 2. **Check the running kubelet process:** Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to view the kubelet process and its arguments. 3. **Verify the `--streaming-connection-idle-timeout` argument:** - If present, ensure it is NOT set to `0`. - If absent, check if a kubelet config file is specified via the `--config` flag. 4. **Inspect the kubelet config file (if applicable):** - Open the config file (path specified by `--config`). - Ensure `streamingConnectionIdleTimeout` is NOT set to `0`. 5. **Expected secure configuration:** - The argument or config value should be set to a non-zero value (e.g., `5m` or the default). 6. **Confirm compliance:** - Document the findings for each node. Compliance is met if all nodes have a non-zero timeout configured.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --protect-kernel-defaults argument is set to true
description: Protect	tuned	kernel	parameters	from	overriding	kubelet	default	kernel	parameter	values.
rationale: Kernel	parameters	are	usually	tuned	and	hardened	by	the	system	administrators	before putting	the	systems	into	production.	These	parameters	protect	the	kernel	and	the	system. Your	kubelet	kernel	defaults	that	rely	on	such	parameters	should	be	appropriately	set	to match	the	desired	secured	system	state.	Ignoring	this	could	potentially	lead	to	running pods	with	undesired	kernel	behavior. Impact: You	would	have	to	re-tune	kernel	parameters	to	match	kubelet	parameters.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify	that	the	--protect-kernel-defaults	argument	is	set	to	true. If	the	--protect-kernel-defaults	argument	is	not	present,	check	that	there	is	a	Kubelet config	file	specified	by	--config,	and	that	the	file	sets	protectKernelDefaults	to	true.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	protectKernelDefaults: true. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS	variable. --protect-kernel-defaults=true Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: 191	|	P a g e systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	--protect-kernel-defaults	is	not	set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 192	|	P a g e
impact: You	would	have	to	re-tune	kernel	parameters	to	match	kubelet	parameters.
references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 192	|	P a g e
justification: The control requires verification of the kubelet process arguments and/or configuration files directly on each Kubernetes node. These settings are not exposed via Alibaba Cloud APIs, Aliyun CLI, or Alibaba Cloud Config. While automation could be partially achieved through custom scripts or orchestration tools (e.g., Ansible), this would still require SSH access to the underlying ECS instances, which is outside the scope of Alibaba Cloud’s native compliance automation capabilities. Therefore, a manual approach is necessary.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into Each Kubernetes Node:** Use SSH to access each worker node in your ACK cluster. 2. **Check Kubelet Process Arguments:** Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to view the kubelet process and its arguments. 3. **Verify --protect-kernel-defaults Argument:** - If `--protect-kernel-defaults=true` is present in the process arguments, the node is compliant. - If not present, proceed to the next step. 4. **Check Kubelet Configuration File:** - Identify if the kubelet is started with a `--config` argument pointing to a configuration file. - Open the referenced config file and verify that `protectKernelDefaults: true` is set. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - The node is compliant if either the command-line argument or the config file sets the value to `true`. - Document your findings for each node. 6. **Repeat for All Nodes:** Ensure every node in the cluster is checked individually.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set to true
description: Allow	Kubelet	to	manage	iptables.
rationale: Kubelets	can	automatically	manage	the	required	changes	to	iptables	based	on	how	you choose	your	networking	options	for	the	pods.	It	is	recommended	to	let	kubelets	manage the	changes	to	iptables.	This	ensures	that	the	iptables	configuration	remains	in	sync	with pods	networking	configuration.	Manually	configuring	iptables	with	dynamic	pod	network configuration	changes	might	hamper	the	communication	between	pods/containers	and	to the	outside	world.	You	might	have	iptables	rules	too	restrictive	or	too	open. Impact: Kubelet	would	manage	the	iptables	on	the	system	and	keep	it	in	sync.	If	you	are	using	any other	iptables	management	solution,	then	there	might	be	some	conflicts.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify	that	if	the	--make-iptables-util-chains	argument	exists	then	it	is	set	to	true. If	the	--make-iptables-util-chains	argument	does	not	exist,	and	there	is	a	Kubelet	config file	specified	by	--config,	verify	that	the	file	does	not	set	makeIPTablesUtilChains	to false.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	makeIPTablesUtilChains: true. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and remove	the	--make-iptables-util-chains	argument	from	the 193	|	P a g e KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS	variable. Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	--make-iptables-util-chains	argument	is	set	to	true. impact: Kubelet	would	manage	the	iptables	on	the	system	and	keep	it	in	sync.	If	you	are	using	any other	iptables	management	solution,	then	there	might	be	some	conflicts.

justification: This control requires verification of the Kubelet process arguments or configuration file on each node to ensure `--make-iptables-util-chains` is set to true. Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or managed services do not expose node-level Kubelet process arguments or configuration file contents for ACK clusters, as these are managed at the OS level within the node and not surfaced through cloud-native interfaces. Both AI opinions recognize the inherent manual nature of this check, and there is currently no Alibaba Cloud-native method to automate this verification.
manual_summary: 1. **Access Each Worker Node:** - SSH into each worker node in your Alibaba Cloud ACK cluster. 2. **Check Kubelet Process Arguments:** - Run the following command to view the running Kubelet process and its arguments: ```bash ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` - Inspect the output for the `--make-iptables-util-chains` argument. - If present, confirm it is set to `true`. 3. **Check Kubelet Configuration File (if used):** - If the Kubelet is started with a `--config` argument, locate and open the referenced configuration file (typically YAML). - Verify that the `makeIPTablesUtilChains` field is either set to `true` or not set to `false`. 4. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - The Kubelet process must either: - Include `--make-iptables-util-chains=true` in its arguments, OR - The configuration file must have `makeIPTablesUtilChains: true` (or not set to false). 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the findings for each node. Compliance is achieved only if all nodes meet the above criteria.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --event-qps argument is set to 0 or a level which ensures appropriate event capture
description: Security	relevant	information	should	be	captured.	The	--event-qps	flag	on	the	Kubelet	can be	used	to	limit	the	rate	at	which	events	are	gathered.	Setting	this	too	low	could	result	in relevant	events	not	being	logged,	however	the	unlimited	setting	of	0	could	result	in	a	denial of	service	on	the	kubelet.
rationale: It	is	important	to	capture	all	events	and	not	restrict	event	creation.	Events	are	an	important source	of	security	information	and	analytics	that	ensure	that	your	environment	is consistently	monitored	using	the	event	data. Impact: Setting	this	parameter	to	0	could	result	in	a	denial	of	service	condition	due	to	excessive events	being	created.	The	cluster's	event	processing	and	storage	systems	should	be	scaled to	handle	expected	event	loads.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Review	the	value	set	for	the	--event-qps	argument	and	determine	whether	this	has	been set	to	an	appropriate	level	for	the	cluster.	The	value	of	0	can	be	used	to	ensure	that	all events	are	captured. If	the	--event-qps	argument	does	not	exist,	check	that	there	is	a	Kubelet	config	file specified	by	--config	and	review	the	value	in	this	location.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	eventRecordQPS:	to	an	appropriate	level. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and 195	|	P a g e set	the	below	parameter	in	KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS	variable. Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default	Value: By	default,	--event-qps	argument	is	set	to	5. impact: Setting	this	parameter	to	0	could	result	in	a	denial	of	service	condition	due	to	excessive events	being	created.	The	cluster's	event	processing	and	storage	systems	should	be	scaled to	handle	expected	event	loads.

justification: While some aspects of Kubernetes configuration can be queried via the Kubernetes API (`kubectl`) or Alibaba Cloud ACK APIs, the specific `--event-qps` argument is a Kubelet process-level flag or a node-local configuration file setting. Alibaba Cloud APIs and the ACK control plane do not expose Kubelet process arguments or node-local configuration files for direct inspection. Therefore, compliance verification requires manual SSH access to each node to inspect the running Kubelet process or its configuration file, as described in the official audit steps.
manual_summary: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes worker node in the ACK cluster. 2. Run the following command to view the Kubelet process and its arguments: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` 3. Inspect the output for the `--event-qps` argument. - If present, verify it is set to `0` (unlimited, not recommended unless event system is scaled) or a value appropriate for your cluster’s event processing capacity. 4. If the `--event-qps` argument is not present in the process, check if the Kubelet is started with a configuration file (look for `--config` argument). 5. If a config file is used, open the file (typically `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` or as specified) and review the `eventRecordQPS` setting. 6. Ensure the value is not set too low (which could miss events) or to `0` unless the event system is properly scaled. The default is `5`; adjust as appropriate for your environment. 7. Document the findings for each node and confirm all are compliant.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
description: Setup	TLS	connection	on	the	Kubelets.
rationale: The	connections	from	the	apiserver	to	the	kubelet	are	used	for	fetching	logs	for	pods, attaching	(through	kubectl)	to	running	pods,	and	using	the	kubelet’s	port-forwarding functionality.	These	connections	terminate	at	the	kubelet’s	HTTPS	endpoint.	By	default,	the apiserver	does	not	verify	the	kubelet’s	serving	certificate,	which	makes	the	connection subject	to	man-in-the-middle	attacks,	and	unsafe	to	run	over	untrusted	and/or	public networks.
audit: Run	the	following	command	on	each	node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify	that	the	--tls-cert-file	and	--tls-private-key-file	arguments	exist	and	they	are	set	as appropriate. If	these	arguments	are	not	present,	check	that	there	is	a	Kubelet	config	specified	by	--config and	that	it	contains	appropriate	settings	for	tlsCertFile	and	tlsPrivateKeyFile.
remediation: If	using	a	Kubelet	config	file,	edit	the	file	to	set	tlsCertFile	to	the	location	of	the	certificate file	to	use	to	identify	this	Kubelet,	and	tlsPrivateKeyFile	to	the	location	of	the corresponding	private	key	file. If	using	command	line	arguments,	edit	the	kubelet	service	file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf	on	each	worker	node	and	set the	below	parameters	in	KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS	variable. --tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file>	--tls-private-key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file> Based	on	your	system,	restart	the	kubelet	service.	For	example: 197	|	P a g e systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service CIS	Controls: Version	6 1.6	Use	Of	Client	Certificates	For	System	Authentication Use	client	certificates	to	validate	and	authenticate	systems	prior	to	connecting	to	the private	network. 14.2	Encrypt	All	Sensitive	Information	Over	Less-trusted	Networks All	communication	of	sensitive	information	over	less-trusted	networks	should	be encrypted.	Whenever	information	flows	over	a	network	with	a	lower	trust	level,	the information	should	be	encrypted. Version	7 1.8	Utilize	Client	Certificates	to	Authenticate	Hardware	Assets Use	client	certificates	to	authenticate	hardware	assets	connecting	to	the	organization's trusted	network. 198	|	P a g e
justification: The verification of the `--tls-cert-file` and `--tls-private-key-file` arguments for Kubelet requires direct inspection of the process arguments or configuration files on each Kubernetes node. Alibaba Cloud APIs, CLI, or Config do not expose the internal configuration or process arguments of Kubelet running inside ECS instances or managed nodes. Both AI opinions agree that SSH access and manual inspection are necessary, and there is no feasible way to automate this check solely through Alibaba Cloud’s native tools.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH to Each Node:** - Use SSH to connect to every node in the Kubernetes cluster where Kubelet is running. 2. **Check Running Kubelet Process:** - Execute `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to view the running Kubelet process and its arguments. 3. **Verify TLS Arguments:** - Confirm that the process includes both `--tls-cert-file` and `--tls-private-key-file` arguments, and that their paths are set to valid certificate and key files. 4. **Check for Kubelet Config File (if arguments not present):** - If the above arguments are not present in the process, check if a `--config` argument is used. - Locate and open the referenced Kubelet config file (commonly `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` or as specified). - Ensure the config file contains valid `tlsCertFile` and `tlsPrivateKeyFile` entries with correct file paths. 5. **Validate File Existence and Permissions:** - Confirm that the referenced certificate and key files exist on disk and have secure permissions (readable only by the Kubelet process). 6. **Document Findings:** - Record which nodes are compliant and which are not, including file paths and permissions.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
description: The	RBAC	role	cluster-admin	provides	wide-ranging	powers	over	the	environment	and should	be	used	only	where	and	when	needed.
rationale: Kubernetes	provides	a	set	of	default	roles	where	RBAC	is	used.	Some	of	these	roles	such	as cluster-admin	provide	wide-ranging	privileges	which	should	only	be	applied	where absolutely	necessary.	Roles	such	as	cluster-admin	allow	super-user	access	to	perform	any action	on	any	resource.	When	used	in	a	ClusterRoleBinding,	it	gives	full	control	over every	resource	in	the	cluster	and	in	all	namespaces.	When	used	in	a	RoleBinding,	it	gives full	control	over	every	resource	in	the	rolebinding's	namespace,	including	the	namespace itself. Impact: Care	should	be	taken	before	removing	any	clusterrolebindings	from	the	environment	to ensure	they	were	not	required	for	operation	of	the	cluster.	Specifically,	modifications should	not	be	made	to	clusterrolebindings	with	the	system:	prefix	as	they	are	required for	the	operation	of	system	components.
audit: Obtain	a	list	of	the	principals	who	have	access	to	the	cluster-admin	role	by	reviewing	the clusterrolebinding	output	for	each	role	binding	that	has	access	to	the	cluster-admin role. kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom- columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name Review	each	principal	listed	and	ensure	that	cluster-admin	privilege	is	required	for	it.
remediation: 202	|	P a g e Identify	all	clusterrolebindings	to	the	cluster-admin	role.	Check	if	they	are	used	and	if	they need	this	role	or	if	they	could	use	a	role	with	fewer	privileges. Where	possible,	first	bind	users	to	a	lower	privileged	role	and	then	remove	the clusterrolebinding	to	the	cluster-admin	role	: kubectl delete clusterrolebinding [name] Default	Value: By	default	a	single	clusterrolebinding	called	cluster-admin	is	provided	with	the system:masters	group	as	its	principal. Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 203	|	P a g e

Specifically,	modifications should	not	be	made	to	clusterrolebindings	with	the	system:	prefix	as	they	are	required for	the	operation	of	system	components.

Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 203	|	P a g e
justification: While the initial retrieval of cluster role bindings and their principals (e.g., via `kubectl` or Alibaba Cloud ACK APIs) can be automated, the core of this control is to assess whether each principal truly requires "cluster-admin" privileges. This assessment requires human judgment based on organizational context, job roles, and operational necessity—information not available via any Alibaba Cloud API or CLI. Therefore, full automation is not feasible for the compliance decision.
manual_summary: 1. **Retrieve ClusterRoleBindings:** - Use the Alibaba Cloud ACK console or connect to your cluster via `kubectl`: ``` kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name ``` - Alternatively, use the Alibaba Cloud Console: ACK > Clusters > [Your Cluster] > RBAC > ClusterRoleBindings. 2. **Identify Principals with "cluster-admin":** - Review the output for all ClusterRoleBindings where `ROLE` is `cluster-admin`. - Note all associated `SUBJECT` values (users, groups, or service accounts). 3. **Assess Necessity:** - For each principal, manually verify with relevant stakeholders (e.g., cluster administrators, security team) whether "cluster-admin" privileges are strictly required for their operational duties. - Document justification for each principal retaining this privilege. 4. **Confirm Secure Configuration:** - Ensure only the minimum necessary principals have "cluster-admin" access. - If excessive or unjustified access is found, remediate by binding users to lower-privileged roles and deleting unnecessary clusterrolebindings.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize access to secrets
description: The	Kubernetes	API	stores	secrets,	which	may	be	service	account	tokens	for	the Kubernetes	API	or	credentials	used	by	workloads	in	the	cluster.	Access	to	these	secrets should	be	restricted	to	the	smallest	possible	group	of	users	to	reduce	the	risk	of	privilege escalation.
rationale: Inappropriate	access	to	secrets	stored	within	the	Kubernetes	cluster	can	allow	for	an attacker	to	gain	additional	access	to	the	Kubernetes	cluster	or	external	resources	whose credentials	are	stored	as	secrets. Impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	secrets	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
audit: Review	the	users	who	have	get,	list	or	watch	access	to	secrets	objects	in	the	Kubernetes API.
remediation: Where	possible,	remove	get,	list	and	watch	access	to	secret	objects	in	the	cluster. Default	Value: By	default	in	a	kubeadm	cluster	the	following	list	of	principals	have	get	privileges	on secret	objects CLUSTERROLEBINDING                                    SUBJECT TYPE            SA-NAMESPACE cluster-admin                                         system:masters Group system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller  clusterrole- aggregation-controller  ServiceAccount  kube-system 204	|	P a g e system:controller:expand-controller                   expand-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:generic-garbage-collector           generic-garbage- collector           ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:namespace-controller                namespace-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:persistent-volume-binder            persistent-volume- binder            ServiceAccount  kube-system system:kube-controller-manager                        system:kube-controller- manager      User CIS	Controls: Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 205	|	P a g e
impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	secrets	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that auditing access to Kubernetes secrets via RBAC in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters can be fully automated. The Kubernetes API, accessible through `kubectl` (which can be invoked via Aliyun CLI or Alibaba Cloud CLI), exposes all RBAC roles and bindings. These can be programmatically parsed to determine which users or service accounts have `get`, `list`, or `watch` permissions on secrets. No manual inspection or console-only steps are required.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) clusters - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl` (via Aliyun CLI or Alibaba Cloud CLI) to run: - `kubectl get roles,rolebindings,clusterroles,clusterrolebindings --all-namespaces -o yaml` - Properties to Check: - In each Role and ClusterRole, look for rules where: - `resources` includes `secrets` - `verbs` includes any of `get`, `list`, or `watch` - Map these roles to users/service accounts/groups via RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings. - Pass Criteria: - Only system-required accounts (e.g., `system:masters`, `clusterrole-aggregation-controller`, and other documented system components) have `get`, `list`, or `watch` access to secrets. - Fail Criteria: - Any non-system user, group, or service account is found with `get`, `list`, or `watch` access to secrets.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles
description: Kubernetes	Roles	and	ClusterRoles	provide	access	to	resources	based	on	sets	of	objects	and actions	that	can	be	taken	on	those	objects.	It	is	possible	to	set	either	of	these	to	be	the wildcard	"*"	which	matches	all	items. Use	of	wildcards	is	not	optimal	from	a	security	perspective	as	it	may	allow	for	inadvertent access	to	be	granted	when	new	resources	are	added	to	the	Kubernetes	API	either	as	CRDs or	in	later	versions	of	the	product.
rationale: The	principle	of	least	privilege	recommends	that	users	are	provided	only	the	access required	for	their	role	and	nothing	more.	The	use	of	wildcard	rights	grants	is	likely	to provide	excessive	rights	to	the	Kubernetes	API.
audit: Retrieve	the	roles	defined	across	each	namespaces	in	the	cluster	and	review	for	wildcards kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o yaml Retrieve	the	cluster	roles	defined	in	the	cluster	and	review	for	wildcards kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml
remediation: Where	possible	replace	any	use	of	wildcards	in	clusterroles	and	roles	with	specific	objects or	actions. CIS	Controls: Version	6 5.1	Minimize	And	Sparingly	Use	Administrative	Privileges Minimize	administrative	privileges	and	only	use	administrative	accounts	when	they	are required.	Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. 206	|	P a g e Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 207	|	P a g e
justification: While some aspects of role and cluster role retrieval can be scripted using `kubectl` or Alibaba Cloud's APIs, the core requirement of this control is to review for wildcard (“*”) usage in permissions, which demands human judgment to assess context and appropriateness. Alibaba Cloud does not currently provide a native, fully automated compliance check or managed rule in Alibaba Cloud Config specifically for detecting and evaluating wildcard use in Kubernetes Roles and ClusterRoles within ACK clusters. Therefore, a manual review is necessary to ensure accurate compliance assessment.
manual_summary: 1. **Retrieve All Roles and ClusterRoles:** - Connect to your ACK cluster using `kubectl`. - Run: `kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o yaml` to export all Roles. - Run: `kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml` to export all ClusterRoles. 2. **Inspect for Wildcards:** - Manually review the YAML output for any occurrence of `"*"` in the `resources`, `verbs`, or `apiGroups` fields within the rules of each Role and ClusterRole. - Pay particular attention to permissions that grant `"*"` access, which means all resources or all actions. 3. **Assess Appropriateness:** - For each wildcard found, determine if its use is strictly necessary (e.g., for system or break-glass roles) or if it can be replaced with more specific permissions. - Document any roles where wildcard usage is not justified by operational need. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Compliance is achieved if no unnecessary wildcards are present in Roles or ClusterRoles. - Record findings and remediation actions as required.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize access to create pods
description: The	ability	to	create	pods	in	a	namespace	can	provide	a	number	of	opportunities	for privilege	escalation,	such	as	assigning	privileged	service	accounts	to	these	pods	or mounting	hostPaths	with	access	to	sensitive	data	(unless	Pod	Security	Policies	are implemented	to	restrict	this	access) As	such,	access	to	create	new	pods	should	be	restricted	to	the	smallest	possible	group	of users.
rationale: The	ability	to	create	pods	in	a	cluster	opens	up	possibilities	for	privilege	escalation	and should	be	restricted,	where	possible. Impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	pods	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
audit: Review	the	users	who	have	create	access	to	pod	objects	in	the	Kubernetes	API.
remediation: Where	possible,	remove	create	access	to	pod	objects	in	the	cluster. Default	Value: By	default	in	a	kubeadm	cluster	the	following	list	of	principals	have	create	privileges	on pod	objects CLUSTERROLEBINDING                                    SUBJECT TYPE            SA-NAMESPACE cluster-admin                                         system:masters Group 208	|	P a g e system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller  clusterrole- aggregation-controller  ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:daemon-set-controller               daemon-set-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:job-controller                      job-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:persistent-volume-binder            persistent-volume- binder            ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:replicaset-controller               replicaset-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:replication-controller              replication-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:statefulset-controller              statefulset-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system CIS	Controls: Version	6 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Version	7 14	Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know Controlled	Access	Based	on	the	Need	to	Know 209	|	P a g e
impact: Care	should	be	taken	not	to	remove	access	to	pods	to	system	components	which	require this	for	their	operation
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that auditing which users have "create" access to pod objects in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters can be performed programmatically. The necessary data (RoleBindings, ClusterRoleBindings, and their associated permissions) is accessible via the Kubernetes API, which can be queried using Alibaba Cloud's CLI tools or kubectl. This allows for a comprehensive and repeatable audit without manual inspection.
automation_summary: - **Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service:** Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) clusters - **API/CLI Method:** - Use `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json` and `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json` (via Alibaba Cloud CLI or kubectl with proper kubeconfig for the ACK cluster) - **Properties to Check:** - In each RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding, examine the `subjects` (users, groups, service accounts) and the referenced `roleRef`. - For each referenced Role or ClusterRole, check if it grants the `create` verb on the `pods` resource. - **Pass Criteria:** - Only system-required accounts (e.g., system:masters, clusterrole-aggregation-controller, and other documented system accounts) and a minimal, justified set of user/service accounts have `create` access to pods. - **Fail Criteria:** - Any non-essential or excessive user/service accounts or groups have `create` access to pods, violating the principle of least privilege.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
description: The	default	service	account	should	not	be	used	to	ensure	that	rights	granted	to applications	can	be	more	easily	audited	and	reviewed.
rationale: Kubernetes	provides	a	default	service	account	which	is	used	by	cluster	workloads	where no	specific	service	account	is	assigned	to	the	pod. Where	access	to	the	Kubernetes	API	from	a	pod	is	required,	a	specific	service	account should	be	created	for	that	pod,	and	rights	granted	to	that	service	account. The	default	service	account	should	be	configured	such	that	it	does	not	provide	a	service account	token	and	does	not	have	any	explicit	rights	assignments. Impact: All	workloads	which	require	access	to	the	Kubernetes	API	will	require	an	explicit	service account	to	be	created.
audit: For	each	namespace	in	the	cluster,	review	the	rights	assigned	to	the	default	service	account and	ensure	that	it	has	no	roles	or	cluster	roles	bound	to	it	apart	from	the	defaults. Additionally	ensure	that	the	automountServiceAccountToken: false	setting	is	in	place	for each	default	service	account.
remediation: Create	explicit	service	accounts	wherever	a	Kubernetes	workload	requires	specific	access to	the	Kubernetes	API	server. justification: This control can be fully audited using Kubernetes API calls, which are accessible via `kubectl` and can be automated in Alibaba Cloud ACK clusters. Both the existence of role bindings for default service accounts and the `automountServiceAccountToken` property can be programmatically checked across all namespaces. Alibaba Cloud's ACK exposes the Kubernetes API, and these checks can be scripted using `kubectl` or Alibaba Cloud's SDKs/CLI, making full automation feasible without manual intervention.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) - Service Accounts and Role Bindings - API/CLI Method: - Use `kubectl get namespaces` to enumerate all namespaces. - For each namespace, use `kubectl get serviceaccount default -n <namespace> -o yaml` to check the `automountServiceAccountToken` property. - Use `kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings -n <namespace> -o yaml` to list all bindings and check if any reference the `default` service account. - Properties to Check: - `automountServiceAccountToken` field in the default service account (should be `false`). - RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding subjects: ensure none are assigned to `ServiceAccount/default` except system defaults. - Pass Criteria: - In every namespace, the default service account has `automountServiceAccountToken: false`. - No custom RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings are assigned to the default service account. - Fail Criteria: - Any namespace where the default service account has `automountServiceAccountToken: true` or is unset. - Any non-default RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding assigned to the default service account.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure that Service Account Tokens are only mounted where necessary
description: Service	accounts	tokens	should	not	be	mounted	in	pods	except	where	the	workload running	in	the	pod	explicitly	needs	to	communicate	with	the	API	server
rationale: Mounting	service	account	tokens	inside	pods	can	provide	an	avenue	for	privilege	escalation attacks	where	an	attacker	is	able	to	compromise	a	single	pod	in	the	cluster. Impact: Pods	mounted	without	service	account	tokens	will	not	be	able	to	communicate	with	the	API server,	except	where	the	resource	is	available	to	unauthenticated	principals.

remediation: Modify	the	definition	of	pods	and	service	accounts	which	do	not	need	to	mount	service account	tokens	to	disable	it. Default	Value: By	default,	all	pods	get	a	service	account	token	mounted	in	them. justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that the configuration for `automountServiceAccountToken` in Kubernetes pods and service accounts can be programmatically queried using standard Kubernetes APIs, which are fully supported in Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK). The necessary data is accessible via `kubectl` or Alibaba Cloud's Kubernetes API endpoints, making automated assessment feasible and reliable.
automation_summary: - Alibaba Cloud Resource/Service: Alibaba Cloud Container Service for Kubernetes (ACK) - Pods and Service Accounts - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` and `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o json` to retrieve pod and service account specifications. - Properties to Check: - For each pod: the `spec.automountServiceAccountToken` field. - For each service account: the `automountServiceAccountToken` field. - Pass Criteria: - Pods and service accounts that do NOT require API server access must have `automountServiceAccountToken: false` explicitly set, or inherit a service account with this property set to `false`. - Fail Criteria: - Pods or service accounts that do NOT require API server access but have `automountServiceAccountToken` set to `true` or left unset (defaulting to `true`), indicating unnecessary token mounting.

source: CIS ALIBABA CLOUD CONTAINER SERVICE FOR KUBERNETES (ACK) BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
description: Do	not	generally	permit	containers	to	be	run	with	the	securityContext.privileged	flag set	to	true.
rationale: Privileged	containers	have	access	to	all	Linux	Kernel	capabilities	and	devices.	A	container running	with	full	privileges	can	do	almost	everything	that	the	host	can	do.	This	flag	exists to	allow	special	use-cases,	like	manipulating	the	network	stack	and	accessing	devices. There	should	be	at	least	one	PodSecurityPolicy	(PSP)	defined	which	does	not	permit privileged	containers. If	you	need	to	run	privileged	containers,	this	should	be	defined	in	a	separate	PSP	and	you should	carefully	check	RBAC	controls	to	ensure	that	only	limited	service	accounts	and users	are	given	permission	to	access	that	PSP. Impact: Pods	defined	with	spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true	will	not	be permitted.
audit: Get	the	set	of	PSPs	with	the	following	command: 214	|	P a g e kubectl get psp For	each	PSP,	check	whether	privileged	is	enabled: kubectl get psp <name> -o=jsonpath='{.spec.privileged}' Verify	that	there	is	at	least	one	PSP	which	does	not	return	true.
remediation: Create	a	PSP	as	described	in	the	Kubernetes	documentation,	ensuring	that	the .spec.privileged	field	is	omitted	or	set	to	false. Default	Value: By	default,	PodSecurityPolicies	are	not	defined. Implement	focused	auditing	on	the	use	of	administrative	privileged	functions	and monitor	for	anomalous	behavior. Version	7 5.1	Establish	Secure	Configurations Maintain	documented,	standard	security	configuration	standards	for	all	authorized operating	systems	and	software. 215	|	P a g e
impact: Pods	defined	with	spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true	will	not	be permitted.
